content.subtitle: Uses
items: The future (for fixed events and schedules)

content.subtitle: Examples
items: The sun **rises** in the east and **sets** in the west.
He **goes** for a run before breakfast three times a week.
She **teaches** mathematics at a secondary school in the city.
I know you don't believe me, but I **do understand** how difficult this is.
The flight **departs** at six fifteen tomorrow morning.
I'll message you once I **arrive** at the hotel.
Martinez **crosses** to Diaz — and Diaz **heads** it into the net!
You **turn** the handle clockwise and **push** the door firmly.
A scientist **walks** into a café and **orders** a cup of H2O2 ...

content.subtitle: Emphatic Present Simple
content.description: We use do/does + bare infinitive to add emphasis, typically to contradict something said or to express strong feeling.
examples: I realise you think I never listen, but I **do listen** to every word you say.
He may look relaxed, but he **does take** his responsibilities very seriously.

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Actions happening at the moment of speaking
Temporary situations and actions happening around now
Annoying or surprising habits (usually with always)

content.subtitle: Examples
items: The children **are playing** football in the park at the moment.
I **am staying** with a friend in Dublin while my flat is being renovated.
More and more people **are choosing** to work remotely these days.
My neighbour **is always playing** loud music late at night!
She enters the café. A man **is reading** a newspaper in the corner ...
**Are you coming** to the team dinner on Friday evening?
I'll start the presentation once everyone **is sitting** down.

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Situations and states that started in the past and are still true now
A series of actions continuing up to now
Completed actions at an unspecified time in the past
Completed actions where the result matters now

content.subtitle: Examples
items: He**'s worked** at the same company for more than a decade.
Our team**'s completed** five reports and submitted three proposals this month.
**Have** you ever **visited** a country where they drive on the left?
They **have fixed** the connection, so the internet is working again.
She**'s just received** a job offer from a company in Berlin.
I'll let you know as soon as I**'ve spoken** to the manager.

content.subtitle: Note on American English
content.description: In American English, the simple past is frequently used in contexts where British English would use the present perfect.
examples: US: **Did** you **finish** the report yet? UK: **Have** you **finished** the report yet?
US: He already **left**. UK: He**'s** already **left**.

examples.note: American English often uses gotten as the past participle of get when expressing the meaning 'obtain', 'become' or 'move'.

examples: US: I **haven't gotten** your invitation yet. UK: I **haven't got** your invitation yet.

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Actions and situations that started in the past and are still continuing (or have just stopped)
Emphasising how long an action has been going on

content.subtitle: Examples
items: She**'s been working** on the quarterly report since early this morning.
We**'ve been renovating** the kitchen for the past three weeks.
He won't feel ready for the interview until he**'s been preparing** for a bit longer.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: The present perfect simple focuses on the number of completed actions or results, while the present perfect continuous emphasises the duration of an ongoing activity.

examples.correct: He**'s written** four articles this week. (number of completed actions)
He**'s been writing** articles all week. (emphasising duration)

sections.title: Stative and Non-Stative Uses of Verbs

content.subtitle: Stative Verbs (generally used in simple tenses)
Watch out!
content.description: Some verbs that are typically stative can function as dynamic verbs when they refer to an activity rather than a condition. Examples: be, consider, feel, have, look, see, smell, taste, think, weigh.

examples.correct: She **has** a meeting every Monday. (state: possession)
She**'s having** lunch with a client right now. (action)
The barista **is tasting** the espresso to adjust the blend. (action)

sections.title: A: Present simple or continuous?
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: The weather has changed quickly. I think it **rains / is raining** heavily in the valley.
The library **opens / is opening** at nine o'clock every weekday morning.
We **don't usually hold / aren't usually holding** staff reviews during the summer months.
My sister **always borrows / is always borrowing** my jacket without asking!
More and more students **choose / are choosing** online courses over traditional classes these days.
Can I ring you back later? I **have / am having** a call with a client at the moment.
The ceremony **takes / is taking** place on the first Sunday of November every year.
Where **do you go / are you going** for lunch today? Have you decided yet?
Researchers **warn / are warning** that sea levels **continue / are continuing** to rise at an alarming rate.
The new restaurant near the station **does / is doing** incredibly well this month.

sections.title: B: Error correction
sections.instructions: Each sentence contains a grammar error in the bold section. Write the correct form.

items.sentence: My boss **lose always** his notes before the weekly meeting — it's very frustrating.
He **is never drinking** tea without adding sugar — he says it tastes too bitter.
The morning post **is arriving** at around eight o'clock every day without fail.
The students **already finish** the first unit of their new coursebook.
Unfortunately, the teacher **is not knowing** the answer to your question.
How long **are you working** at this organisation? You must know everyone by now.
She **is studying** medicine at university — that's her permanent degree programme.
They **are owning** three shops in different parts of the city.

sections.title: C: Present perfect simple & continuous
sections.instructions: Complete the sentences using the correct form of the verb in brackets. Use the present perfect simple or present perfect continuous.

items.sentence: This is the first time (I / ever / visit) ...... a country outside Europe.
(you / ever / work) ...... night shifts in your previous job?
The athletes (prepare) ...... for the competition all week — they deserve a rest.
How many languages (she / learn) ...... in her life? I know she studied in several countries.
items.answer: has she learned / has she learnt

items.sentence: I (wait) ...... for this appointment for weeks — I'm relieved it's finally today.
We (not / decide) ...... on a name for the new product yet.
(the engineers / test) ...... the bridge all day, but the work is not yet complete.
That's the second time today (he / forget) ...... to close the kitchen window.
I can't believe (you / never / try) ...... skiing before! You should book a trip.
The unemployment rate (drop) ...... steadily over the past few months.

sections.title: D: Adverbs of time
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap to complete the sentences.

items.sentence: Have you ...... tried a dish that you couldn't identify a single ingredient in?
I've ...... sent the email — you should receive it within a few minutes.
He's been training with the national team ...... the beginning of the season.
They've been queuing ...... two hours to get tickets for the concert.
Have you replied to the invitation ......? The deadline is tomorrow.
She's ...... received a response from the university — she's still checking her inbox.
So far this season, the player has ...... scored a single goal.
This is the first time I've ...... spoken on live radio.
He's ...... read eight books this year and is halfway through a ninth.
Have you heard anything ...... about the job application? I'm keeping my fingers crossed.

sections.title: E: Multiple choice
sections.instructions: Choose the correct answer: A, B, C or D.

questions.sentence: He (1) ...... on an important presentation this week, so he can't meet until Friday.
Glass (2) ...... at a much higher temperature than most metals.
The government (3) ...... the minimum wage twice already this year.
Something's wrong — I think the heating (4) ...... all night, which explains the heat.
The post office (5) ...... at one o'clock on Saturdays, so you'd better hurry.
Look outside — the river (6) ...... higher. I think there's been heavy rain upstream.
She (7) ...... the contract twice and she still has some questions.
(8) ...... you ever ...... a formal complaint to a transport company?
My sister (9) ...... yoga every morning — it's part of her daily routine.
The research team (10) ...... new data all week and they're close to a conclusion.

sections.title: F: Stative verbs (cloze)
sections.instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct form of a stative verb from the box. Use each verb once.

items.sentence: Everyone (1) ...... that a healthy diet can help prevent many diseases.
This laptop (2) ...... to the IT department — it shouldn't be in here.
I (3) ...... whether the train will arrive on time in this weather.
The bread (4) ...... wonderful — did you bake it yourself?
Each tablet (5) ...... a full day's recommended amount of vitamin C.
How much (6) ...... a monthly gym membership ...... in this city?
Many customers (7) ...... to pay by card rather than cash these days.

sections.title: G: Matching (Stative verbs)
sections.instructions: Match each sentence beginning (1–8) with the correct ending (A–H).

items.sentence: I think **B** that this new policy will benefit everyone involved.
items.answer: B – that this new policy will benefit everyone involved.

items.sentence: My nephew resembles **H** his grandfather — same build, same walk.
items.answer: H – his grandfather — same build, same walk.

items.sentence: This book belongs **F** to the school library, not to me.
items.answer: F – to the school library, not to me.

items.sentence: The package contains **G** three sets of batteries and a charger.
items.answer: G – three sets of batteries and a charger.

items.sentence: I weigh **A** slightly less than I did before I started the diet.
items.answer: A – slightly less than I did before I started the diet.

items.sentence: The bakery smells **D** wonderful in the early morning.
items.answer: D – wonderful in the early morning.

items.sentence: This issue matters **E** a great deal to the local residents.
items.answer: E – a great deal to the local residents.

items.sentence: I'm still considering **C** whether to change careers at my age.
items.answer: C – whether to change careers at my age.

sections.title: H: Error correction (extra words)
sections.instructions: Each sentence contains one extra word that should not be there. Click the extra word.

items.sentence: I've been to sitting at my desk all day without a single break.
He has been already sent his application to three different companies.
They have just been arrived from a long flight and need some rest.
Have you ever [have] thought about taking up a new sport or activity?
This is the very first time that she has ever travelled abroad on her own.
She is been practising the piano all morning and her hands are tired.
The report has already been submitted it to the committee last Friday.
We still haven't yet chosen a destination for the annual company trip.
I know that he has been training very hard since from the start of term.
They are living there ever since they came back from their trip abroad.

sections.title: I: Cloze
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap to complete the text.

items.sentence: Remote working (1) ...... become increasingly common over the past few years.
Many companies (2) ...... been offering flexible arrangements to their staff since the pandemic.
The number of employees who choose to work from home (3) ...... grown year on year.
Many workers say they (4) ...... been more productive since they stopped commuting daily.
The government (5) ...... just announced a new scheme to support home workers.
Research shows that flexible working (6) ...... a significant impact on employee wellbeing.
How long (7) ...... this trend been reshaping the modern workplace?
So far, surveys (8) ...... already shown that staff value flexibility above many other benefits.

sections.title: J: Key word transformation
sections.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first, using the word given. Do not change the word given. Use between two and five words.

items.sentence:
This is my first trip to Japan.
**NEVER**
I ...... Japan before.

She took her first driving lesson six months ago and is still taking lessons.
**FOR**
She ...... six months.
items.answer: has been learning to drive for

items.sentence:
The last time she went to the gym was two weeks ago.
**BEEN**
She ...... two weeks.
items.answer: hasn't been to the gym for

items.sentence:
They bought that house and they are still living there.
**SINCE**
They ...... they bought it.

It's irritating that my colleague keeps interrupting me when I'm speaking.
**ALWAYS**
My colleague ...... me when I'm speaking.

The first bus to the airport leaves at six fifteen.
**DEPARTS**
The first bus to the airport ...... at six fifteen.

This is the best campaign we have worked on.
**EVER**
This is the best campaign we ...... on.
